slides.title: Assessing the Xtensr Labs
slides.subtitle: Physical Vulnerabilities: applying the assessment method to Scenario 2A — walk the new lab's plans, identify physical vulnerabilities, and document each as high, moderate, or low risk
slides.footerLeft: AP Cybersecurity · College Board CED (Effective Fall 2026)
slides.script: Welcome to Lesson 2.2, Day 4. The last three days gave you the tools: the named attacks from 2.2.A, the harms from 2.2.B, and the high-moderate-low method from 2.2.C. Today you use all of it on a real job. This is Scenario 2A, Securing Xtensr Labs. You work on the physical security team at Xtensr Research Labs; the company is acquiring a smaller research lab across town, and you've been tasked with conducting a physical vulnerability assessment of the new building. Today covers the first two of your four scenario tasks: review the building plans and current controls to identify physical vulnerabilities, and assess the risk from each one. Recommending controls and monitoring comes later, in Topics 2.3 and 2.4. So today you are the assessor — walk the plans, name the vulnerabilities, and document a risk level for every one, just like a working security analyst.

slides.heading: Your Assignment: Assess the New Lab
slides.subheading: The CB unit scenario, verbatim in setup — you are on the physical security team.
slides.context: You work on the physical security team at Xtensr Research Labs. Xtensr is buying a smaller research company in the same town to expand their operations. You have been tasked with conducting a physical vulnerability assessment of the new lab.
tasks: Review the building plans and current security controls for this new lab and identify physical vulnerabilities
Assess the risk from the vulnerabilities identified
(Topic 2.3) Recommend physical security controls to mitigate these vulnerabilities
(Topic 2.4) Recommend the placement of monitoring equipment to detect physical security breaches
slides.note: Today (Lesson 2.2) covers the first two tasks: IDENTIFY the vulnerabilities and ASSESS the risk. The last two tasks come in Topics 2.3 and 2.4.
slides.script: Here is your assignment, straight from the unit scenario. You work on the physical security team at Xtensr Research Labs. Xtensr is buying a smaller research company in the same town to expand operations, and you've been tasked with conducting a physical vulnerability assessment of the new lab. The full scenario has four tasks across the whole unit: review the plans and identify physical vulnerabilities, assess the risk from them, then later recommend controls and recommend monitoring placement. Those last two are Topics 2.3 and 2.4 — not today. Today, in Lesson 2.2, you do the first two: identify the vulnerabilities and assess each one's risk. That's the analyst's core job, and it's exactly the method you built yesterday. Let's walk the building.

slides.heading: What You’ll Be Filling In
slides.subheading: Your Day 4 Guided Notes have two sections that match the assessment workflow. Both are CB-required.

sections.title: Walk the Plans: Identify Vulnerabilities
sections.description: Tour the new lab room by room, naming each physical vulnerability with the attack or harm it enables (EK 2.2.A.2–A.6, B.3–B.5).

sections.title: Assess and Document Each Risk
sections.description: Grade every identified vulnerability high, moderate, or low using the two-factor method, and record it in an assessment table (EK 2.2.C.1–C.4).

slides.note: Fill in your Guided Notes as we go. The Lesson 2.2 page on apcsexamprep.com has every answer if you fall behind.
slides.script: Two sections today, both CB-required and both about doing the assessment. First, walk the plans: tour the new lab room by room and name each physical vulnerability you find, tying it to the attack or harm it enables from the last three days. Second, assess and document: grade every vulnerability high, moderate, or low using the two-factor method, and record it in an assessment table. Keep the packet open — today's notes become a model assessment you can study from for the exam.

slides.heading: By the End of Today You Will...

items.text: Walk the Scenario 2A Xtensr building plans and identify each physical vulnerability with the attack or harm it enables (LO 2.2.C)
Grade every identified vulnerability high, moderate, or low and record it in a documented assessment table (LO 2.2.C)
Justify each risk grade with the two factors — asset sensitivity/value and likelihood of exploitation (LO 2.2.C)

slides.script: Three things you'll be able to do by the end of today, all under learning objective 2.2.C — now applied to the Scenario 2A building. First, walk the Xtensr lab plans room by room and identify each physical vulnerability, tying every one to the attack or harm it enables from the last three days. Second, grade each vulnerability high, moderate, or low and record it in a documented assessment table — the actual deliverable the unit scenario asks for. Third, justify each grade with the two factors, asset sensitivity or value and likelihood of exploitation. By the end of class you'll have walked an entire facility, identified its vulnerabilities, and produced a documented high-moderate-low assessment — the exact work product the scenario asks for and the reasoning the exam rewards.

slides.label: Walk the Plans
slides.script: Section seven of the lesson. Learning objective 2.2.C, applied. We tour the new Xtensr lab room by room and identify the physical vulnerabilities — using everything from Days 1 and 2 to name them. Twelve minutes.

slides.heading: Touring the New Lab: Where Are the Weaknesses?
slides.subheading: The acquired lab's plans reveal vulnerabilities tied straight to the attacks and harms you've studied.

left.title: The Perimeter and Entrances
examples: A single badge door serves a busy loading area — easy to piggyback with a large delivery (EK 2.2.A.2)
The same door is propped during deliveries, inviting tailgating (EK 2.2.A.3)
Trash and recycling dumpsters sit in an unfenced lot — dumpster diving (EK 2.2.A.5)
A keypad by the main lab faces an open parking lot — shoulder surfing risk (EK 2.2.A.4)

right.title: Inside: Devices and Power
examples: The server holding research data sits in a room with no lock, off an unmonitored hallway (EK 2.2.B.4)
Lab workstations have exposed, accessible USB ports (EK 2.2.B.5)
The electrical panel is in an unsecured utility closet — power disruption (EK 2.2.B.3)
A reception PC on the internal network is reachable by any visitor (foothold)

slides.footer: ASSESSMENT STEP 1 — IDENTIFY: Walk every space and name the vulnerability AND the attack or harm it enables. You can't grade a risk until you've named what's exposed and how it could be exploited.
slides.script: Let's walk the acquired lab and find the weaknesses, using the names you already know. Start at the perimeter and entrances, on the left. There's a single badge door at a busy loading area — a perfect spot to piggyback in behind a large delivery, EK 2.2.A.2 — and staff prop that door during deliveries, which invites tailgating, EK 2.2.A.3. The dumpsters sit in an unfenced lot, so dumpster diving is open, EK 2.2.A.5. And a keypad by the main lab faces the parking lot, exposed to shoulder surfing, EK 2.2.A.4. Now inside, on the right. The server with research data is in a room with no lock off an unmonitored hallway — that's the setup for stealing or copying sensitive data, EK 2.2.B.4. Lab workstations have exposed USB ports, the EK 2.2.B.5 keylogger-or-malware risk. The electrical panel is in an unsecured utility closet, so power disruption is on the table, EK 2.2.B.3. And a reception PC on the internal network is reachable by any visitor. That's step one of the assessment: identify each vulnerability and the attack or harm it enables. Write them all — we grade them next.

slides.label: Assess and Document
slides.script: Section eight. Same objective, 2.2.C, applied. Now we grade what we found. Every vulnerability gets a level — high, moderate, or low — and a one-line justification. Fourteen minutes.

slides.heading: The Xtensr Lab Risk Assessment (Documented)
slides.subheading: Each identified vulnerability graded with the two-factor method and tied to its EK.
rows: Research-data server in an unlocked room off an unmonitored hallway
Steal/copy sensitive data; bypass technical controls (EK 2.2.B.4, C.1)
HIGH (EK 2.2.C.2)
Sensitive asset + no restricted/controlled access
Unsecured electrical panel in an open utility closet
Disrupt power; device and services unavailable (EK 2.2.B.3)
HIGH (EK 2.2.C.2)
Critical system exposed; takes the whole lab down
Reception PC on internal Wi-Fi with exposed USB ports
Foothold to internal resources via malicious port access (EK 2.2.B.5)
MODERATE (EK 2.2.C.3)
Noncritical itself, but a foothold to bigger resources
Propped badge door / loading dock
Piggybacking and tailgating into the building (EK 2.2.A.2, A.3)
MODERATE (EK 2.2.C.3)
Enables entry that could reach sensitive areas
Unfenced dumpsters in the lot
Dumpster diving for useful information (EK 2.2.A.5)
LOW (EK 2.2.C.4)
Lower-value exposure; exploitation less likely
slides.footer: ASSESSMENT STEP 2 — ASSESS & DOCUMENT: Grade with the two dials (asset sensitivity/value × likelihood). HIGH = sensitive/critical + open. MODERATE = a foothold to more. LOW = low value + unlikely. The documented table IS the deliverable.
slides.script: Now we grade and document — step two of the assessment, and the table is your actual deliverable. Top, the research-data server in the unlocked room: sensitive asset, no controlled access, and physical access here bypasses the technical controls — that's the textbook high, EK 2.2.C.2. Next, the unsecured electrical panel: it's a critical system, and disrupting it takes the whole lab's services down, so that's high too, EK 2.2.C.2 by way of the power harm in 2.2.B.3. The reception PC with exposed USB ports on the internal network: the machine itself isn't critical, but it's a foothold inward — that's the definition of moderate, EK 2.2.C.3. The propped badge door: it enables piggybacking and tailgating that could reach sensitive areas, also moderate. And the unfenced dumpsters: real, but lower value and less likely to be the decisive exploit, so low, EK 2.2.C.4. Notice every grade rests on the two dials — sensitivity and likelihood — and every row cites its EK. That documented table is what the security team uses to decide what to fix first. That's the whole job.

slides.heading: The ‘Find Everything, Grade Nothing’ Trap
slides.misconception: A good physical assessment is just a long list of every weakness you can spot in the building.
slides.whyItHappens: Identifying vulnerabilities feels like the hard part, so it's tempting to stop there. A long list looks thorough and complete on its own.
slides.correction: The objective is assess AND document (LO 2.2.C). An unranked list doesn't tell the team where to start. The assessment is only useful once each vulnerability carries a documented risk level — high, moderate, or low (EK 2.2.C.2–C.4) — with a justification tied to asset value and likelihood. Identification is step one; grading and documenting is what makes it an assessment.
slides.example: At the Xtensr lab, listing 'unlocked server room, exposed USB ports, dumpsters in the lot' is just inventory. The value comes from grading them: the unlocked customer-data server is HIGH and gets attention first; the dumpsters are LOW and can wait. Without the grades, a team might tether down low-risk items while the high-risk server stays open — the exact prioritization failure EK 2.2.C is designed to prevent.
slides.script: Here's the trap on the applied day. Finding vulnerabilities feels like the work, so students hand in a long list and call it an assessment. But the objective is assess and document, not just identify. A raw list doesn't tell the team where to start — and at a real facility you can't fix everything at once. The assessment only earns its name when every item carries a documented risk level with a justification tied to asset value and likelihood. At Xtensr, 'unlocked server room, exposed USB ports, dumpsters' is just inventory; the value is in grading them — the customer-data server is high and goes first, the dumpsters are low and can wait. Skip the grading and you might cable down cheap items while the high-risk server stays open. Identification is step one; grading and documenting is the deliverable the scenario actually asks for.

slides.heading: Key Vocabulary (LO 2.2.C, applied)

terms.definition: The Scenario 2A deliverable: reviewing a building's plans and controls to identify physical vulnerabilities and assign each a documented risk level (EK 2.2.C.2).
Walking the plans to name each physical vulnerability and the attack or harm it enables, the necessary precursor to grading risk (EK 2.2.C.1).
Grading each identified vulnerability high, moderate, or low by asset value and likelihood, and recording it so a team can prioritize (EK 2.2.C.2).
An exposed sensitive or critical system without controlled access, like the unlocked research-data server off an unmonitored hallway (EK 2.2.C.2).
A noncritical asset that is a foothold to other resources, like the reception PC with exposed USB ports on the internal network (EK 2.2.C.3).
A low-value, unlikely-to-be-exploited weakness, like the unfenced dumpsters in the parking lot (EK 2.2.C.4).

slides.script: Six terms, all framed around the assessment you just did. The big one is physical vulnerability assessment — the Scenario 2A deliverable itself, EK 2.2.C.2. Then the two steps: identify, walking the plans to name each vulnerability, EK 2.2.C.1; and assess and document, grading and recording each one, EK 2.2.C.2. The last three are your worked findings — a high finding like the unlocked server, EK 2.2.C.2; a moderate finding like the foothold reception PC, EK 2.2.C.3; and a low finding like the dumpsters, EK 2.2.C.4. Knowing these by example is how you'll recognize the levels on the exam. All six in your notes with EK numbers.

slides.heading: Stop and Think — Independent Writing
slides.subheading: Both questions map to LO 2.2.C, applied to Scenario 2A. Complete on your Guided Notes packet.
prompts: Pick any TWO vulnerabilities from the Xtensr assessment table that received DIFFERENT risk levels. For each, name the vulnerability, cite its risk-level, and justify the grade using the two factors (asset sensitivity/value and likelihood of exploitation).
The lab's reception PC is on the internal network with exposed USB ports. Explain why this is graded MODERATE rather than low or high idea of a foothold.
slides.directions: Independent. 6 minutes. Full sentences. I will collect Guided Notes at the end of class.
slides.script: Two questions, six minutes, independent, full sentences. Question one: pick any two Xtensr findings with different grades — say the high server and the low dumpsters — name each, cite its risk EK, and justify with the two factors. Question two: the reception PC — explain why it's moderate, not low or high, using the foothold idea from EK 2.2.C.3: it's noncritical itself but a stepping stone to bigger resources. Be precise; this is exactly the reasoning the scenario and exam want to see.

slides.script: Everything is on the site. Re-run the Lesson 2.2 self-check, then Exercise 7, where you complete the Xtensr lab assessment table for the spaces we didn't grade in class, and Exercise 8, where you write the justification for each grade. The quiz is your five-question topic check tomorrow on Day 5. If you want today again at your own pace, the lesson video walks the whole assessment slide for slide.

slides.heading: End of Day 4
todayPoints: Took on the Scenario 2A role: assessed the newly acquired Xtensr lab (LO 2.2.C)
STEP 1 — identified physical vulnerabilities room by room, tying each to its attack or harm (2.2.A, 2.2.B)
STEP 2 — graded and documented each as high, moderate, or low with a two-factor justification (EK 2.2.C.1–C.4)
Produced a documented risk assessment table — the deliverable the unit scenario asks for
nextPoints: Review and practice across all three objectives — LO 2.2.A, 2.2.B, 2.2.C
Consolidate the named attacks, the harms, and the risk method in one place
slides.teaser: You've named the attacks, explained the harms, learned the method, and run a full assessment. Tomorrow we tie it all together and prove it — review, cumulative practice, and the Lesson 2.2 quiz.
slides.script: Day 4 done. You stepped into the Scenario 2A role and did the analyst's real job: you walked the Xtensr lab, identified its physical vulnerabilities in step one, and in step two graded and documented each as high, moderate, or low with a justification — producing the assessment table the unit scenario asks for. That's LO 2.2.C in action. Tomorrow is Day 5: review and practice across all three objectives, a cumulative scenario, and then the Lesson 2.2 quiz. Review your Guided Notes tonight — make sure you can both identify a vulnerability and justify its risk grade, because the quiz spans the whole topic. Bring it tomorrow.
